title: Applied Python Projects: Object-Oriented Apps
subtitle: Build multifile Python apps with encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, tests, and a final capstone.
intendedFor: Learners who already know Python fundamentals, functions, imports, files, and lists, and now need a strong multifile OOP project course.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Complete modules in order. Each module depends on the last: class files and encapsulation, then inheritance/polymorphism/abstraction, then testing/refactoring, then the final capstone.
courseOverview.summary: Learners build increasingly realistic multifile Python OOP apps while practicing the four pillars: encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism.

moduleSummary.title: Object-Oriented Foundations
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Create class files, constructors, methods, imports, and encapsulated validation.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build an Account Tracker with models, helpers, data, and main.py.

moduleSummary.title: Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Use base classes, subclasses, overrides, mixed collections, and simple interface contracts.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build a polymorphic catalog app with Book and Video subclasses.

moduleSummary.title: Testing and Debugging OOP Projects
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Test object state, subclass overrides, services, storage, imports, and refactors.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Harden an OOP catalog app with tests and regression coverage.

moduleSummary.title: Final OOP Capstone Project
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Integrate object modeling, encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, services, storage, reports, tests, and documentation in one complete application.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Complete a Neighborhood Pantry Request Coordinator with models, services, CSV storage, reports, tests, and README notes.

authoringGuidance: This course must always use subjectSlug python, courseSlug applied-python-projects, catalogSlug python, and profileId python.
The generated draft subject slug is python--applied-python-projects--draft; do not confuse that with the authoring subjectSlug/courseSlug split.
This course uses browser-python-files-runner and python-browser-workspace across all modules; it is a multifile editor workspace course, not terminal-first.
Every technical sketch must have a required embedded try-it code_input with tryItPlacement all_sketches unless a topic is explicitly conceptualOnly.
Embedded try-it exercise IDs, tryIt.id, and tryIt.exerciseKey must use the try-<topic-id>-sketchN convention, for example try-inheritance-for-shared-behavior-sketch0.

Embedded try-it exercises must include full starterFiles, solutionFiles, workspace.solutionFiles, recipe.solutionFiles, and recipe.solutionCode for the expected workspace; do not make try-it less complete than practice/project exercises.
Projects and capstones must be progressive: step N+1 starterFiles must equal step N full solutionFiles, and every step must include all files needed to run that step.

Do not put generated outputs such as reports/summary.txt in requiredFiles. Generated outputs may be verified through runtime/output expectations and may appear in solutionFiles only when the reveal should show the final state.
Use __init__.py files when package imports require them, and include them in starterFiles/solutionFiles when relevant.
Quizzes are conceptual and must not use code_input; graded programming belongs in try-it, practice, module project, or capstone steps.
The four OOP pillars must be taught cumulatively: encapsulation in module 8, inheritance/polymorphism/abstraction in module 9, tested/refactored in module 10, and integrated in module 11.
Do not introduce inheritance, polymorphism, or abstraction before module 9. Do not require OOP tests before module 10.
Keep main.py thin; put domain behavior in models/, orchestration/reporting in services/, sample inputs in data/, checks in tests/, and generated summaries in reports/.
Prefer deterministic fixed_tests or behavior checks that run the whole solution workspace. Avoid visible harness code such as ast.literal_eval or hidden parser helpers in starterCode.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: Two or three topics per week, with one cumulative module project after each standard module and one final capstone module.
recommendedCourseDeliverables: Embedded try-it exercises on every technical sketch
Progressive module projects with chained solution snapshots
Tests for models, services, subclass overrides, and polymorphic behavior
Portfolio-style OOP capstone with README documentation
toolingSuggestions: Multifile workspace with models/, services/, data/, tests/, reports/

moduleMilestones.learnersCan: Create class files, import them, and validate state changes through methods.
Use base classes, subclasses, overrides, and mixed collections.
Test and refactor multifile object projects safely.
Build a complete project using all four OOP pillars.

assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Learners finish with a complete Neighborhood Pantry Request Coordinator capstone and a strong foundation for larger Python applications.

notes: Start every OOP idea through model files and object state.
Teach encapsulation before any inheritance language.
Use multifile starterFiles/solutionFiles even for embedded try-it exercises.
Use simple base classes before optional abc language.
Every polymorphism exercise should show a service or loop that calls the same method on mixed subclasses.
Testing now targets objects, subclasses, polymorphic services, data fixtures, and output files.
Keep generated output files out of requiredFiles.
Final capstone must use all four OOP pillars.
Every capstone step must chain from the previous full solutionFiles snapshot.
Generate full starterFiles and solutionFiles for any code_input exercise.

modules.title: Object-Oriented Foundations
modules.description: Build class files, constructors, methods, and encapsulated object state across a small multifile app.
modules.purpose: Teach classes as the foundation for multifile Python apps and introduce encapsulation as the first OOP pillar.
learningObjectives: Define classes in dedicated model files and import them into main.py.
Use constructors, attributes, and methods to keep object behavior with object state.
Apply encapsulation with validation and helper/service files.
guidedExercises: Create model files and import classes from main.py.
Add constructors and methods that change object state.
Move formatting and validation behavior into helper/service files.
modules.moduleProject: Build a multifile account tracker where each step grows the previous full solution.

sections.title: Object Foundations
sections.description: Start with classes in separate files, object instances, constructors, and state.

topics.title: Class Files and Instances
topics.summary: Create a class in its own file, import it, and make separate objects from it.
learningGoals: Create a model file such as models/profile.py or models/student.py.
Import a class into main.py and create multiple instances.
Keep class code out of the main script when the model deserves its own file.

topics.title: Constructors and Object State
topics.summary: Use __init__ to receive starting data and store state on each object.
learningGoals: Use __init__ parameters to set instance attributes.
Explain how two objects can share a class but keep different state.
Write small checks that prove object state was initialized correctly.

sections.title: Encapsulation and Services
sections.description: Add methods, validation, and helper files so each object owns its behavior.

topics.title: Methods and Responsibility
topics.summary: Move behavior into methods and keep main.py as a thin coordinator.
learningGoals: Use self inside methods to read and update object state.
Decide whether code belongs in a model, service, helper, or main.py.
Refactor repeated formatting into helper files.

topics.title: Encapsulation and Validation
topics.summary: Protect object state with methods that validate changes before saving them.
learningGoals: Use methods such as deposit(), add_score(), or mark_complete() to control state changes.
Reject invalid values before they corrupt object state.
Use a service/helper file to operate on a list of objects without breaking encapsulation.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build an account tracker one cumulative step at a time.

topics.title: Module 8 Account Tracker Project
topics.summary: Create a multifile account tracker with a model file, formatting helper, data file, and thin main script.
learningGoals: Build a complete Account model in models/account.py.
Use helpers/formatting.py and data/accounts.txt with a thin main.py.
Carry each project step's full solution into the next step's starter workspace.

modules.title: Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction
modules.description: Extend the object model with shared base classes, overrides, mixed collections, and simple interface thinking.
modules.purpose: Teach the remaining OOP pillars cumulatively after learners already understand class files, state, methods, and encapsulation.
learningObjectives: Use inheritance to share behavior across related classes.
Override methods when subclasses need specialized behavior.
Use polymorphism and simple abstraction so services can work with mixed object types.
guidedExercises: Create base and subclass model files.
Override behavior while reusing parent initialization.
Build services that loop over mixed object types through shared method names.
modules.moduleProject: Build a catalog app with a base item class, subclasses, polymorphic services, and data loading.

sections.title: Inheritance and Overrides
sections.description: Use parent classes for shared behavior and subclasses for specialization.

topics.title: Inheritance for Shared Behavior
topics.summary: Create subclasses that reuse a base class instead of copying shared code.
learningGoals: Create a parent class such as LibraryItem or Membership.
Create subclasses that inherit shared attributes and methods.
Use imports across models/base.py and subclass files.

topics.title: Overriding and Specialization
topics.summary: Override a parent method when a subclass needs a different result.
learningGoals: Override a method in a subclass without rewriting the whole class.
Use super() when a subclass should reuse parent initialization.
Compare shared behavior with specialized behavior.

sections.title: Polymorphism and Abstraction
sections.description: Use common method names and base-class contracts to keep services flexible.

topics.title: Polymorphic Collections
topics.summary: Loop over different object types and call the same method name on each object.
learningGoals: Store different subclasses in one list.
Call shared method names such as summary(), fee(), or send() on each object.
Write service functions that depend on behavior, not exact class names.

topics.title: Abstraction With Base Interfaces
topics.summary: Use a simple base class/interface idea to define what every object must provide.
learningGoals: Define a small common interface through base-class methods.
Keep reports and services focused on what objects can do.
Avoid overusing inheritance when composition or helpers are clearer.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build a polymorphic catalog one cumulative step at a time.

topics.title: Module 9 Catalog Polymorphism Project
topics.summary: Build a multifile catalog with a base item, Book and Video subclasses, service functions, abstract fee behavior, and loaded data.
learningGoals: Model shared catalog behavior with inheritance.
Use polymorphic services for mixed Book and Video objects.
Carry each project step's full solution into the next step's starter workspace.

modules.title: Testing and Debugging OOP Projects
modules.description: Test object state, subclass behavior, polymorphic services, imports, fixtures, and refactors in multifile apps.
modules.purpose: Turn OOP projects into reliable software by testing behavior, debugging imports/state, and refactoring safely across files.
learningObjectives: Write tests for object state changes, validation rules, and subclass overrides.
Debug import errors and object-state bugs in multifile workspaces.
Refactor main.py into model/service/test files while preserving behavior.
guidedExercises: Write tests in tests/check_*.py for model methods.
Debug shared mutable state and import path mistakes.
Refactor reporting logic into service files.
modules.moduleProject: Add tests, services, storage, and regression coverage to an OOP catalog app.

sections.title: Testing OOP Behavior
sections.description: Write tests for methods, state changes, subclass overrides, and polymorphic loops.

topics.title: Testing Object State
topics.summary: Use assert-based tests to prove object construction and method state changes work.
learningGoals: Write tests that construct objects and inspect attributes.
Test methods that update state such as deposit(), apply_payment(), or mark_done().
Cover invalid input and edge cases.

topics.title: Testing Inheritance and Polymorphism
topics.summary: Test subclass overrides and services that process mixed object lists.
learningGoals: Test inherited behavior and overridden methods.
Test polymorphic service functions with mixed subclasses.
Use small fixtures in tests/check_models.py or tests/check_services.py.

sections.title: Debugging and Refactoring
sections.description: Fix import/state problems and move code into services without changing behavior.

topics.title: Debugging Imports and State
topics.summary: Find broken imports, shared-list bugs, and confusing object state in multifile projects.
learningGoals: Read tracebacks caused by bad module paths or missing __init__.py files.
Fix shared mutable defaults and accidental class-level state.
Use focused print/debug checks only while diagnosing a problem.

topics.title: Refactoring OOP Services
topics.summary: Move reporting and orchestration logic out of main.py into service modules.
learningGoals: Keep main.py thin by moving reusable logic into services/.
Preserve output while moving code across files.
Use tests to protect behavior during refactors.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Harden an OOP project with tests, storage, and regression coverage.

topics.title: Module 10 OOP Quality Project
topics.summary: Add model tests, service tests, storage loading, and final regression coverage to a multifile OOP app.
learningGoals: Create tests/check_*.py files for models and services.
Verify polymorphic behavior and storage loading.
Carry each project step's full solution into the next step's starter workspace.

modules.title: Final OOP Capstone Project
modules.description: Deliver one cumulative OOP capstone that proves abstraction, encapsulation, inheritance, overriding, polymorphism, storage, reporting, testing, and documentation.
modules.purpose: Complete one portfolio-style multifile OOP application where every capstone milestone starts from the previous full solution.
learningObjectives: Design a clear file architecture for a multifile OOP application.
Use encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism in one coherent request-management domain.
Add CSV storage, services, reports, regression checks, and documentation to finish a polished capstone.
guidedExercises: Build the base pantry request model and starter data flow.
Add specialized food and hygiene request subclasses plus an encapsulated service.
Load CSV data, generate deterministic reports, add regression checks, and complete the README handoff.
modules.moduleProject: Complete one six-step Neighborhood Pantry Request Coordinator inside one capstone section and one capstone topic.

sections.title: Final OOP Capstone
sections.description: Complete one portfolio-ready OOP application through six cumulative milestones inside a single topic.

topics.title: Neighborhood Pantry Request Coordinator
topics.summary: Build one cumulative application with an abstract request contract, validated subclasses, a polymorphic service and report, CSV storage, regression tests, and final documentation.
learningGoals: Use `ABC` and `@abstractmethod` to define the request interface.
Use validated properties, inheritance, overriding, and polymorphism in the domain model.
Keep service, storage, reporting, testing, and orchestration responsibilities separated.
Carry one complete multi-file workspace through all six capstone steps.

projectBrief.scenario: A neighborhood pantry needs one dependable application for recording food and hygiene requests, marking fulfilled requests, loading existing requests from CSV, and producing a clear daily handoff report.
projectBrief.role: Junior Python application developer completing a portfolio-ready community-service tool
projectBrief.workspace: Browser multifile Python workspace with models/, services/, storage/, reports/, tests/, data/, main.py, and README.md
projectBrief.deliverable: One six-step cumulative Neighborhood Pantry Request Coordinator with an abstract `PantryRequest` contract, validated concrete subclasses, a mixed polymorphic collection, encapsulated service behavior, CSV loading, deterministic reports, regression checks, and a documented final handoff.
requirements: The capstone module must contain exactly one section and that section must contain exactly one topic.
Keep every milestone runnable from main.py with complete starterFiles and solutionFiles.
Use the previous full solutionFiles as the next milestone starter workspace.
Define PantryRequest with ABC and @abstractmethod; the final project must not use a concrete-only base class.
Use validated properties to protect household, priority, subtype details, and fulfillment state.
Require FoodRequest and HygieneRequest to inherit from PantryRequest and override the abstract interface.
Process a mixed request collection polymorphically through shared methods instead of type-specific report duplication.
Keep collection changes inside PantryService, raw-row translation inside storage, and output composition inside reports.
Add regression assertions that verify the abstract contract, validation failures, mixed-subclass behavior, service state changes, and deterministic output.
Keep regression tests in tests/check_requests.py and run them from the final application without extra output.
Finish with README documentation that explains abstraction, encapsulation, inheritance, overriding, polymorphism, and the run command.

stepLadder.title: Define the Abstract Request Contract
stepLadder.requirement: Create PantryRequest with ABC, @abstractmethod, validated properties, shared state behavior, a concrete summary(), and the first FoodRequest implementation.

stepLadder.title: Add a Second Subclass and Prove Polymorphism
stepLadder.requirement: Add HygieneRequest and process FoodRequest and HygieneRequest together through one shared interface in a mixed collection.

stepLadder.title: Encapsulate the Collection in PantryService
stepLadder.requirement: Protect request storage, validate additions, delegate state changes, count open requests, and build polymorphic summary lines.

stepLadder.title: Rebuild Domain Objects from CSV
stepLadder.requirement: Translate CSV rows into the correct concrete request objects while preserving validation and saved state.

stepLadder.title: Add Polymorphic Reporting and Regression Tests
stepLadder.requirement: Build type-agnostic reports and tests that prove the base class is abstract, invalid state is rejected, and mixed subclasses work together.

stepLadder.title: Complete the OOP Portfolio Handoff
stepLadder.requirement: Finish final orchestration and README documentation, run regression checks, print deterministic output, and end with the readiness line.

learningGoals: Define a real abstract base-class contract with `ABC` and `@abstractmethod`.
Protect shared state with validated properties and service-owned collection behavior.
Use inheritance, overriding, and polymorphism across a mixed request collection.
Integrate CSV storage, deterministic reports, regression tests, and README documentation.

description: A cumulative, project-first Python course where learners build object-oriented apps across multiple files and finish with a complete OOP capstone.

notes: Every topic should produce multifile practice when it is technical.
Try-it cards use try-* exercise keys; regular practice can use ci-*; module project/capstone steps keep descriptive project step ids.
Full-scope solution workspace is required for every reveal and every golden validation contract.
Project and capstone solutionFiles are complete snapshots, not diffs.

globalRules: Every technical sketch must have a required embedded try-it unless explicitly conceptualOnly.
Embedded try-it IDs and exercise keys must start with try- and follow try-<topic-id>-sketchN.
Every code_input exercise must include full starterFiles and full solutionFiles when it uses a workspace.
solutionFiles must include unchanged starter files too; solutionFiles are snapshots, not diffs.
Module project and capstone step N+1 starterFiles must equal step N solutionFiles.
Do not place generated output files such as reports/summary.txt in requiredFiles.

notes: Teach class files, constructors, methods, object state, encapsulation, validation, imports, and helpers.
Do not introduce inheritance, polymorphism, or abstraction yet.
Use multifile try-it exercises from the start: main.py plus models/ or helpers/.
Teach inheritance, overriding, polymorphism, and abstraction/base-class contracts cumulatively.
Use models/base_item.py, subclass files, and services that operate on mixed object lists.
Testing/debugging/refactoring exercises must target multifile OOP apps.
Use tests/check_*.py, services/, data fixtures, and import/state debugging.
Final capstone must integrate encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism.
Each capstone step must carry forward the previous complete workspace.
